entry.title: ChannelNewsAsia: 30 May, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • LTA to train, certify technicians in EV maintenance and servicing
  • Evicted villagers pay a high price for India's hydropower push
  • Shell greenlights $2.5 billion Crux gas project off Australia
  • France's sports ministry to meet police, UEFA over Champions League final chaos
  • 'If you do it your way, I slap you': Woman gets jail for repeatedly assaulting maid
  • Total wage growth rebounded to 3.9% in 2021, but real growth in salaries dampened by higher inflation
  • Wreckage of plane with 22 on board found in Nepal mountains
  • China eyes security pact in Pacific Island summit
  • China's Seazen plans to issue 7.95%, 364-day green bond - term sheet
  • Crooning for a good cause: Gentle Bones set to hold live charity concert in July
  • BOJ's Kuroda vows to patiently continue powerful monetary easing
  • Oil climbs ahead of EU meeting on Russia sanctions
  • Dollar drifts toward monthly loss as Fed bets ease
  • Moomoo provides millennials and Gen Zs easy access to US stocks trading
  • NATO has right to deploy in eastern Europe: Deputy chief
  • Learning Chinese is easier, when lessons come to life
  • Australia unveils bumper summer schedule as COVID recovery continues
  • Ericsson a fifth wheel no more after Indy 500 win
  • Asia stocks hit 3-week top, dollar struggles

  • Commentary: Forrest Li losing billions in net worth is par for the course in the tech world
  • PKR polls: Saifuddin concedes fight for No 2 spot in Malaysian opposition party, congratulates Rafizi

  • Italy's TIM, CDP sign off preliminary accord on single network plan

  • Thousands of Real Madrid fans celebrate Champions league title with team

  • Zverev stutters but recovers to reach French Open last eight

  • US Open finalist Fernandez stays on track of overall improvement

  • Rangnick will not take up Manchester United consultancy role

  • Biden grieves with Texas town as anger mounts over school shooting
  • Ukraine's Donbas 'unconditional priority' for Moscow: Russia's Lavrov
  • Commentary: US has far more gun crime than countries with similar gun ownership rates
  • Monkeypox presents moderate risk to global public health: WHO
  • First hurricane of the eastern Pacific season approaches southern Mexico
  • Colombians head to polls in divisive presidential election
  • Ukraine's Zelenskyy visits frontline in first official appearance outside Kyiv since invasion

  • Death toll mounts from Brazil downpours as search continues

entry.title: Washington Post: 30 May, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • In Colombia, two anti-establishment candidates head to a runoff
  • Severodonetsk under assault as Kremlin forces continue to target Ukraine’s east
  • Ukraine suffers on battlefield while pleading for U.S. arms
  • Agatha grows to hurricane, poses dangerous flood threat to Mexico
  • Nepal pauses rescue effort for missing passenger flight, citing weather
  • Prince William takes center stage at Queen Elizabeth II’s jubilee rehearsal
  • U.N. human rights chief disappoints Uyghur advocates on visit to China
  • Wreckage of plane with 22 on board found in Nepal mountains
  • 1st hurricane of 2022, Agatha heads for Mexico tourist towns
  • Colombia presidential race to runoff; leftist vs businessman

  • The gun debate is paralyzed by our past
  • Abbott vows change after a school shooting. We’ve heard that before.

  • Truth emerges about Chinese repression of Uyghurs — no thanks to the U.N.
  • Despite the war in Ukraine, Biden understands China matters most

  • Our military members need access to all health care

Breaking news and analysis on politics, business, world national news, entertainment more. In-depth DC, Virginia, Maryland news coverage including traffic, weather, crime, education, restaurant reviews and more.

entry.title: South China Morning Post: 30 May, 2022
entry.summary: South China Morning Post: 30 May, 2022 published at 2022-05-30 03:39AM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Vaccine reluctance: why some Hongkongers are still shunning Covid-19 jabs despite coming tightened rules
  • Hong Kong NGO urges government to raise minimum wage, calls for annual review to keep up with inflation
  • All Hong Kong residents to get ‘fortune bags’ containing gifts marking 25th anniversary of city’s return to Chinese rule
  • Amid Shanghai lockdown and economic woes, how can Hong Kong best help China?
  • Public appeal for yearly review of Hong Kong’s minimum wage dismissed as ‘unfeasible’ by city’s labour minister
  • Hong Kong’s John Lee expected to meet Chinese vice-premier to discuss government restructuring, national security, housing
  • Hong Kong must be prepared for geopolitical risks, finance chief warns, as he hits out at US for interfering in global market
  • Coronavirus: clusters at 2 Hong Kong nightspots spark calls for no further easing of social-distancing rules in city’s bars and clubs
  • China finds uranium at ‘impossible’ depth: scientists
  • The earliest bird got the worm: new study finds a 120-million-year-old group of birds probably ate insects rather than fish
  • What is driving China’s ‘assertiveness’ in the South China Sea?
  • Big-spending Shanghai internet celebrity of Ferrari-crash fame revealed to be son of Hong Kong investor
  • Unanswered phones, missed signals: fear of accidental US-China crisis grows
  • Xi Jinping invokes China’s past to rally confidence in its future as country vies with West
  • China-US divide over UN human rights chief’s visit to Xinjiang
  • Problematic maths figures? Review ordered after row over primary school textbook illustrations
  • What we know so far about the newly discovered forest in a massive Chinese sinkhole
  • US views on China ‘gone seriously awry’, Chinese foreign minister warns during Pacific tour
  • Pope Francis names 21 new cardinals, including from Singapore and India as Catholic Church tilts towards Asia
  • China, Sri Lanka’s ‘all-weather friend’, is now displaying low-key approach to island’s economic meltdown – which could upend IMF debt discussions
  • Wreckage of plane found in Nepal mountains, fate of 22 people on board unknown
  • Sri Lanka PM invites protesting youth to join governance under proposed political reforms
  • Philippines President-elect Marcos Jnr chooses stability in naming new cabinet to revive economy and restore family name
  • Philippines: 7 missing after boat collision
  • Coronavirus: in Singapore, aviation jobs are up for grabs as tourism in the city state rebounds
  • Search paused for Nepal plane missing in mountains with 22 on board
  • ‘I’m not a devious villain’: black men in Japan speak out about racial profiling
  • As China’s Wang Yi visits Fiji, Albanese says previous Australian government ‘dropped the ball’ on the Pacific
  • North America’s Chinatowns are going through a revival after pandemic, anti-Asian violence
  • Ex-guerilla and millionaire maverick in race for Colombian presidency
  • Ukrainian defenders hold out in Donbas city under heavy Russian fire
  • Concerns raised over police aggression toward Liverpool fans at Paris Champions League final
  • Shooting at Oklahoma festival leaves 1 dead, 7 injured
  • US Justice Department to review police response to Texas school shooting
  • Volkswagen accused of ‘slavery’ practices under Brazil dictatorship during 1970s and 1980s
  • Texas school massacre town begs Joe Biden to ‘do something’
  • Clashes at Al-Aqsa mosque and skirmishes during Israeli flag march in Jerusalem’s Muslim quarter
  • Texas shooting: Families whose children survived turn to crowdfunding for therapy
  • Russia, Serbia agree 3-year gas supply contract: ‘best terms in Europe’
  • Russia will only use nuclear weapons if its existence is threatened, says Kelin, ambassador to Britain
  • Zelensky visits Ukraine’s frontline in east for first time since invasion began
  • Gustavo Petro, the ex-rebel front runner in Colombian vote, could shake US ties
  • ‘Now I am a beggar’: fleeing the Russian advance in Ukraine
  • Hong Kong stocks rise to 4-week high with gains in Meituan, Alibaba as Shanghai prepares to exit lockdown
  • Hong Kong’s Insurance Authority faces talent shortage amid fierce competition during travel restrictions, emigration
  • Hong Kong gets new climate-linked stock market tracker amid big fund inflows, policy controversy
  • US fund manager piles US$1.2 billion into Alibaba, JD.com, Chinese tech peers in 12 months waiting for policy, valuation payoff
  • ESG – a new opportunity for engaging talent
  • Guangdong’s Shaoguan city holds first conference for China’s big data integration of eastern and western regions
  • How Bangladesh has surged past India on road to South Asian economic stardom
  • Start-up founders joining Finnish relocation programme want to grow Hong Kong brand
  • How the climate crisis generation can usher in an era of responsible capitalism
  • It’s important to prepare children for the potential dangers of the metaverse
  • Record-breaking Indian cycling mum on her recent 6,000km tandem ride and proving the doubters wrong
  • How the ‘kimchi war’ between China and South Korea could have been avoided
  • Japan wakes up to microaggressions and unconscious bias directed at minorities, and senses it’s time to change
  • A peek inside League of Legends creator Riot Games’ Los Angeles HQ, and why it’s not like your average workplace
  • Vignettes from a side of China we don’t often see in Chinese-Canadian author’s short story collection Taobao
  • How to age well: tennis, dancing, massage and meditation keep Malaysian grandma happy and active at 78
  • History of real pirates who dressed as passengers to hijack and plunder Hong Kong ships in the South China Sea
  • Richest Real Housewives of Atlanta cast members – net worths, ranked, for season 14: from TLC and Destiny’s Child songwriter Kandi Burruss to ex-Miss USA Kenya Moore
  • Radhika Merchant’s most expensive outfits: cute D&G sandals, casual Chanel and Hermès combos, and Sabyasachi Mukherjee gowns – the ‘future Mrs Anant Ambani’ sure knows how to glam up
SCMP.com, Hong Kong's premier online English daily provides exclusive up-to-date news, audio video news, podcasts, RSS Feeds, Blogs, breaking news, top stories, award winning news and analysis on Hong Kong and China.

Articles in this issue:

  • The Donald and the Kremlin Don: how Trump’s toxic legacy helps Putin | Simon Tisdall

The Asahi Shimbun is widely regarded for its journalism as the most respected daily newspaper in Japan. The English version offers selected articles from the vernacular Asahi Shimbun, as well as extensive coverage of cool Japan,focusing on manga, travel and other timely news.

Wired is a full-color monthly American magazine, published in both print and online editions, that reports on how emerging technologies affect culture, the economy and politics. Monthly edition, best run at the start of every month.

entry.title: Nature: 26 May, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:

  • Sinking diatoms trap silicon in deep seawater of acidified oceans
  • Breakthrough in teleportation furthers quantum network development
  • From the archive: Jamaican coral reefs, and indispensable photography
  • Volatile neurons unite to stabilize visual experience
  • Metabolic diversity drives cancer cell invasion
  • Evolution of Earth’s tectonic carbon conveyor belt
  • Defining the risk of SARS-CoV-2 variants on immune protection
  • In situ recording of Mars soundscape
  • Free-electron lasing with compact beam-driven plasma wakefield accelerator
  • Qubit teleportation between non-neighbouring nodes in a quantum network
  • Realizing repeated quantum error correction in a distance-three surface code
  • Demonstration of fault-tolerant universal quantum gate operations
  • Cilia metasurfaces for electronically programmable microfluidic manipulation
  • Cobalt-electrocatalytic HAT for functionalization of unsaturated C–C bonds
  • Enhanced silica export in a future ocean triggers global diatom decline
  • Hmx gene conservation identifies the origin of vertebrate cranial ganglia
  • Mosquito brains encode unique features of human odour to drive host seeking
  • Emergent reliability in sensory cortical coding and inter-area communication
  • Transcriptomic mapping uncovers Purkinje neuron plasticity driving learning
  • Extricating human tumour immune alterations from tissue inflammation
  • Intracellular lipid surveillance by small G protein geranylgeranylation
  • Intermittent PI3Kδ inhibition sustains anti-tumour immunity and curbs irAEs
  • PHGDH heterogeneity potentiates cancer cell dissemination and metastasis
  • Transcriptional coupling of distant regulatory genes in living embryos
  • Maternal inheritance of glucose intolerance via oocyte TET3 insufficiency
  • Molecular basis for the initiation of DNA primer synthesis
  • Author Correction: Fluctuating interaction network and time-varying stability of a natural fish community
  • Author Correction: The loss of ions from Venus through the plasma wake
  • Publisher Correction: Reproducible brain-wide association studies require thousands of individuals
Nature is a weekly international multidisciplinary scientific journal publishing peer-reviewed research in all fields of science and technology on the basis of its originality, importance, interdisciplinary interest, timeliness, accessibility, elegance and surprising conclusions. Nauture also provides rapid, authoritative, insightful and arresting news and interpretation of topical and coming trends affecting science, scientists and the wider public.

Popular Science. Monthly magazine. Should be downloaded around the middle of each month.

entry.title: Asian Review of Books: 30 May, 2022
entry.summary: Asian Review of Books: 30 May, 2022 published at 2022-05-30 00:33AM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • “I Was the President Mistress!!” by Miguel Syjuco
  • New Book Announcement: “Ethical Encounters: Transnational Feminism, Human Rights, and War Cinema in Bangladesh” by Elora Halim Chowdhury
  • “In the Forest of the Blind: The Eurasian Journey of Faxian’s Record of Buddhist Kingdoms” by Matthew W King
  • “Solo Dance” by Li Kotomi
  • Podcast with Kerry Brown, co-author of “China Through European Eyes: 800 Years of Cultural and Intellectual Encounter”
  • New Book Announcement: “Into the Desert” by Xuemo
  • Here be Dragons: Asian myths and fables inspiring recent Young Adult Literature
  • “Ogata Kōrin: Art in Early Modern Japan” by Frank Feltens
  • New Book Announcement: “Beethoven in Beijing: Stories from the Philadelphia Orchestra’s Historic Journey to China” by Jennifer Lin
  • “Silent Parade” by Keigo Higashino
In addition to reviewing books about or of relevance to Asia, the Asian Review of Books also features long-format essays by leading Asian writers and thinkers, to providing an unparalleled forum for discussion of key contemporary issues by Asians for Asia and a vehicle of intellectual depth and breadth where leading thinkers can write on the books, arts and ideas of the day. Widely quoted and referenced, with an archive of more than one thousand book reviews, it is the only web resource dedicated to Asian books. And now, with the addition of the new premium content, the Asian Review of Books, is a must-read publication.

Literary review publishing essay-length book reviews and topical articles on politics, literature, history, philosophy, science and the arts by leading writers and thinkers
